vulnerability.title: Certificate status checking in SSSD
vulnerability.description: Multifactor solutions that require devices separate from information systems gaining access include, for example, hardware tokens providing time-based or challenge-response authenticators and smart cards. Configuring certificate_verification to ocsp_dgst= ensures that certificates for multifactor solutions are checked via Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP).
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q sssd-common; then var_sssd_certificate_verification_digest_function='' # sssd configuration files must be created with 600 permissions if they don't exist # otherwise the sssd module fails to start OLD_UMASK=$(umask) umask u=rw,go= MAIN_CONF="/etc/sssd/conf.d/certificate_verification.conf" found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "$MAIN_CONF /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/conf.d/*.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[sssd\]([^\n\[]*\n+)+?[[:space:]]*certificate_verification" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "certificate_verification=ocsp_dgst=$var_sssd_certificate_verification_digest_function" "$f"; then

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits SSSD configuration files to enable OCSP certificate verification and sets proper file permissions. This is a standard authentication hardening change with minimal impact, requiring only a service restart and not a system reboot. The risk of lockout is low if the change is applied correctly.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential temporary authentication disruption; misconfiguration could cause SSSD failure

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
No existing config, so create a new file under /etc/sssd/conf.d.  
Certificate verification is a domain option, so add it to the domain section.  
Use [domain/default] for simplicity and set `certificate_verification = ocsp_dgst`.  
Set file permissions to 600 (chmod 600) to protect credentials.  
Restart sssd (systemctl restart sssd) to apply the change.

review_feedback: The plan creates a conf.d file with a [domain/default] section and sets certificate_verification=ocsp_dgst, but SSSD expects the setting under the [sssd] section (or a correctly formatted domain section) and requires a digest algorithm (e.g., ocsp_dgst=sha256). Without these, OCSP verification will not be enabled, so the vulnerability remains.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan creates a conf.d file with a [domain/default] section and sets certificate_verification=ocsp_dgst, but SSSD expects the setting under the [sssd] section (or a correctly formatted domain section) and requires a digest algorithm (e.g., ocsp_dgst=sha256). Without these, OCSP verification will not be enabled, so the vulnerability remains.
concerns: Incorrect configuration section; should be under [sssd] or a properly formatted domain section.
Missing digest algorithm after ocsp_dgst=, which may cause SSSD to ignore the setting.
Potential overwriting of existing configuration files without backup.
suggested_improvements: Create the file with a [sssd] section (or [domain/yourdomain]) and set certificate_verification=ocsp_dgst=sha256 (or the desired digest).
Backup existing conf.d files before creating or modifying them.
Validate the new configuration with sss -d or sssctl configcheck before restarting the service.
Ensure the file is owned by root and permissions are set to 600.
